item.title: RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships 2026: Apply for Prestigious Research Positions in Australia
item.dc:creator: Ning Ma
item.description:
Applications are now open for the RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships 2026, a prestigious programme designed to attract outstanding local and international researchers who want to conduct applied, transdisciplinary research with measurable benefits for society, the economy and the environment. Each year, RMIT University appoints talented researchers and research teams to work on complex real-world challenges. […]

The post RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships 2026: Apply for Prestigious Research Positions in Australia appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Applications are now open for the RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships 2026, a prestigious programme designed to attract outstanding local and international researchers who want to conduct applied, transdisciplinary research with measurable benefits for society, the economy and the environment.

Each year, RMIT University appoints talented researchers and research teams to work on complex real-world challenges. The fellowship programme provides successful applicants with the institutional support, professional development opportunities and research environment needed to build influential academic careers and translate research into practical impact.

For the 2026 recruitment round, RMIT is seeking applicants whose work aligns with three strategic research areas:

  • Regenerative and Sustainable Futures
  • MedTech Innovation
  • Digital Innovation

The fellowships are available across four categories covering academic levels A to D. Applicants must hold a PhD or equivalent doctoral qualification and satisfy the specific eligibility requirements for their chosen fellowship category.

Applications close at 11:59 a.m. AEST on Wednesday, 29 July 2026.

About the RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships Program

The RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships Program is part of the university’s long-term strategy to strengthen research and innovation with real-world impact.

The programme seeks researchers who can work across traditional academic boundaries and collaborate with industry, government, communities and other research partners. Fellows are expected to contribute not only to academic knowledge but also to solutions that address major social, environmental, technological and economic challenges.

RMIT is particularly interested in researchers who demonstrate:

  • Excellence within their academic or professional field
  • Creativity and imagination in research design
  • Strong potential for interdisciplinary collaboration
  • A commitment to producing impact beyond academia
  • Alignment with RMIT’s strategic research priorities
  • An ability to build connections across disciplines, organisations and communities

Appointments will be made on merit, with particular attention given to the applicant’s alignment with the nominated research focus area and its associated high-priority topics.

Research Priority Areas for the 2026 Recruitment Round

The 2026 fellowships focus on three broad areas intended to support RMIT’s research and innovation ambitions.

Regenerative and Sustainable Futures

This research area focuses on advancing regenerative and sustainable approaches that restore, renew and revitalise environmental, economic and social systems.

Research may contribute to the development of new ways of responding to challenges such as environmental degradation, climate change, resource pressures, social inequality and unsustainable production or consumption.

Applicants working in this area should demonstrate how their research could contribute to more resilient and sustainable futures.

Relevant approaches may involve collaboration across fields such as:

  • Environmental science
  • Sustainability
  • Engineering
  • Architecture and urban development
  • Social sciences
  • Economics
  • Design
  • Policy
  • Climate research
  • Circular and regenerative systems

Applicants should consult the official fellowship information for the specific high-priority topics accepted under this research area.

MedTech Innovation

The MedTech Innovation priority seeks research that can transform health outcomes through advances in medical technologies.

The focus is on innovation that improves the prevention, diagnosis, monitoring or treatment of health conditions and strengthens the wider healthcare system.

Potential research may involve areas such as:

  • Medical devices
  • Health technologies
  • Biomedical engineering
  • Diagnostics
  • Digital health
  • Therapeutic innovation
  • Health data
  • Patient-centred technology
  • Translational medical research

Applicants should explain how their research can move beyond theory and contribute to improved health outcomes, healthcare delivery or medical practice.

Digital Innovation

The Digital Innovation priority supports multidisciplinary research that contributes to a prosperous, secure and responsible digital future.

RMIT is seeking research that responds to the opportunities and risks created by rapid technological development.

Relevant work may relate to:

  • Artificial intelligence
  • Cybersecurity
  • Digital systems
  • Data science
  • Emerging technologies
  • Digital economies
  • Human-computer interaction
  • Responsible technology
  • Digital inclusion
  • Technology governance

Successful proposals should demonstrate strong research quality while addressing the broader social, economic, ethical or security implications of digital transformation.

Fellowship Categories and Academic Levels

The RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships are available through four categories covering academic levels A to D.

These levels represent different stages of an academic research career. The programme may therefore include opportunities for researchers ranging from early-career scholars to highly experienced research leaders.

The exact eligibility rules, expected achievements and research experience requirements differ by fellowship category.

Applicants should carefully review the relevant category before applying, particularly in relation to:

  • Years since completion of the PhD
  • Academic level
  • Research track record
  • Publication record
  • Leadership experience
  • Industry or community engagement
  • Evidence of research impact
  • Ability to attract research funding
  • Career interruptions or special circumstances

Applicants should not assume that holding a PhD alone guarantees eligibility for every category.

Benefits of the RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships

Successful fellows will receive access to a supportive research environment designed to help them build strong and sustainable careers.

The programme offers:

Prestigious Research Fellowship Appointments

Fellowships are available across academic levels A to D, allowing researchers at different career stages to compete within an appropriate category.

Research Support Funding

Successful fellows may receive generous research support funding.

The amount and conditions depend on the selected fellowship category. Applicants should consult the category-specific information for exact details.

Access to RMIT Research Facilities

Fellows will be able to use RMIT’s research facilities, institutional resources and specialist infrastructure relevant to their work.

Interdisciplinary Networks

RMIT has more than 40 interdisciplinary networks developed through its eight Enabling Impact Platforms.

These networks create opportunities for fellows to:

  • Collaborate across academic disciplines
  • Connect with researchers working on related challenges
  • Build industry and community partnerships
  • Develop joint research projects
  • Increase the reach and relevance of their research

Mentoring

Fellows will receive mentoring to support their research development, career planning and integration into the RMIT research environment.

Researcher Development Programmes

Successful applicants will have access to professional development programmes designed to help researchers identify new pathways to impact.

These programmes may support skills related to research leadership, collaboration, communication, knowledge translation and engagement beyond academia.

Flexible and Supportive Workplace

RMIT describes itself as a recognised employer of choice with a progressive, inclusive and values-based culture.

The university emphasises flexibility, ethical research, diversity and a supportive working environment.

Why Conduct Research at RMIT?

RMIT University places strong emphasis on research that produces meaningful outcomes outside the university.

The institution’s research environment is characterised by:

  • A focus on excellence and impact
  • Strong industry and community engagement
  • A commitment to translating research into practical outcomes
  • Opportunities for interdisciplinary collaboration
  • A global outlook
  • Civic responsibility
  • Support for diverse research topics and impact pathways
  • A progressive and inclusive institutional culture

RMIT’s approach may be particularly attractive to researchers who want to collaborate with partners outside academia and apply their expertise to practical challenges.

Who Can Apply?

The fellowships are open to both:

  • Australian researchers
  • International researchers

Applicants may apply from any country, provided they meet the required academic and category-specific criteria.

The programme is intended for outstanding researchers whose work aligns with the 2026 research priorities and who can demonstrate the potential to make a positive real-world contribution.

General Eligibility Requirements

All applicants must:

  1. Hold a PhD, doctorate or equivalent doctoral qualification.
  2. Complete and submit the application form before the 2026 deadline.
  3. Meet the additional eligibility criteria for the selected fellowship category.
  4. Demonstrate strong alignment with one of the three strategic research areas.
  5. Show evidence of research excellence and potential impact.

RMIT employees who currently hold ongoing academic appointments are not eligible for the Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships.

Applicants holding other forms of employment or association with RMIT should review the official eligibility rules to confirm whether they may apply.

Selection Considerations

Appointments will be made on merit.

Applications are expected to be assessed according to factors such as:

  • Research quality
  • Academic achievements
  • Alignment with RMIT’s strategic research priorities
  • Potential for societal, environmental or economic impact
  • Creativity and originality
  • Interdisciplinary potential
  • Career stage and fellowship category
  • Ability to collaborate with external partners
  • Evidence of research translation
  • Potential contribution to RMIT’s research environment

Applicants should clearly demonstrate how their proposed research fits the selected priority area rather than relying on a broad or indirect connection.

How to Prepare a Strong Application

A competitive application should clearly explain:

1. The Research Problem

Identify the specific challenge or research gap the proposed work will address.

2. Alignment With the Priority Area

Explain how the proposal fits Regenerative and Sustainable Futures, MedTech Innovation or Digital Innovation.

3. Research Approach

Describe the methods, theoretical framework and interdisciplinary elements of the proposed work.

4. Expected Impact

Show how the research could create benefits beyond academic publication.

These benefits may relate to:

  • Society
  • Public policy
  • Communities
  • Industry
  • Healthcare
  • Technology
  • The environment
  • Economic development

5. Applicant’s Track Record

Provide evidence of relevant research achievements, publications, collaborations, leadership and engagement.

6. Contribution to RMIT

Explain how the applicant will contribute to RMIT’s research culture, interdisciplinary networks and strategic goals.

7. Career Development

Describe how the fellowship will support the applicant’s development and future research leadership.

Applicants should also ensure that their materials address the requirements of the correct academic category.

Application Process

Applications for the 2026 round must be submitted through the official RMIT application process.

Before applying, candidates should:

  • Review the four fellowship categories
  • Confirm their eligibility
  • Select the most appropriate research priority
  • Read the high-priority topics under that research area
  • Prepare all required documents
  • Complete every section of the application form
  • Submit before the stated deadline

Applicants should rely on the official application page for the complete list of required documents and any instructions concerning references, research proposals, CVs or supporting materials.

APPLY HERE

Application Deadline

Applications close at:

11:59 a.m. Australian Eastern Standard Time on Wednesday, 29 July 2026

International applicants should convert the deadline into their local time zone and submit early. The closing time is in the morning under AEST, not at the end of the calendar day.

Late or incomplete applications may not be considered.

Conclusion

The RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships 2026 offer an important opportunity for talented researchers seeking to build influential careers while addressing major global challenges.

Through generous research support, access to facilities, mentoring, interdisciplinary networks and professional development, the programme provides an environment in which fellows can pursue ambitious applied research.

Researchers working in Regenerative and Sustainable Futures, MedTech Innovation or Digital Innovation are encouraged to review the category-specific requirements and prepare applications that clearly demonstrate excellence, alignment and real-world impact.

Applications must be submitted by 11:59 a.m. AEST on Wednesday, 29 July 2026.

For further information and application requirements, CLICK HERE.

To discover more fellowship opportunities, VISIT HERE.

The post RMIT Vice-Chancellor’s Research Fellowships 2026: Apply for Prestigious Research Positions in Australia appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Canvas Home Small Grants 2026 for Artisan Groups in Developing Countries | Up to $1,500 in Funding for Craft Businesses and Cooperatives
item.dc:creator: Bob Waudo
item.description:
Artisan groups and craft-producing organizations in developing countries now have an exciting opportunity to access financial support through the Canvas Home Small Grants 2026 program. Administered by HANDMADE to MARKET (HtoM), a division of Creative Learning, this initiative is designed to strengthen artisan enterprises by providing funding for materials, equipment, training, marketing, and other business […]

The post Canvas Home Small Grants 2026 for Artisan Groups in Developing Countries | Up to $1,500 in Funding for Craft Businesses and Cooperatives appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Artisan groups and craft-producing organizations in developing countries now have an exciting opportunity to access financial support through the Canvas Home Small Grants 2026 program. Administered by HANDMADE to MARKET (HtoM), a division of Creative Learning, this initiative is designed to strengthen artisan enterprises by providing funding for materials, equipment, training, marketing, and other business development activities.

The program offers grants of up to $1,500 to eligible artisan cooperatives, community-based organizations, and small craft businesses seeking to expand employment opportunities, improve production, preserve traditional crafts, and generate sustainable income.

If your artisan organization is looking for financial assistance to scale production, improve product quality, access new markets, or strengthen business operations, this grant could provide the support needed to grow sustainably.

Application Deadline: August 17, 2026, at 11:59 PM Eastern Standard Time (EST).

About HANDMADE to MARKET (HtoM)

HANDMADE to MARKET (HtoM) is a division of Creative Learning, a Washington, D.C.-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization dedicated to empowering artisan businesses around the world.

The organization works with artisan communities by providing:

  • Access to international markets
  • Business development training
  • Eco-effective production methods
  • Design innovation
  • Marketing support
  • Strategic partnerships
  • Community-building initiatives

Through these efforts, HtoM seeks to promote sustainable economic growth while preserving cultural heritage and improving the livelihoods of artisan communities worldwide.

Canvas Home Small Grants 2026 Overview

The Canvas Home Small Grants program exists to strengthen established artisan groups by helping them obtain the resources necessary to improve production, increase sales, and create more employment opportunities.

Each successful applicant may receive funding of up to $1,500.

Rather than supporting individual artisans or new businesses, the program focuses on strengthening existing craft-producing organizations that already have an established structure and are ready to grow.

The grants help artisan groups become more competitive while preserving traditional craftsmanship and promoting environmentally responsible production practices.

Grant Value

Successful applicants may receive grants of up to $1,500.

Funding is intended to help artisan organizations:

  • Expand employment opportunities
  • Increase production capacity
  • Improve product quality
  • Reach new markets
  • Increase income generation
  • Strengthen long-term sustainability

Types of Grants Available

Applicants may apply for only one of the following grant categories.

1. Materials, Tools, Equipment, and Instruction Grant

This funding category supports improvements in craft production through the purchase of essential resources and technical support.

Eligible activities include:

  • Purchasing raw materials
  • Buying specialized craft tools
  • Purchasing production equipment
  • Establishing raw material banks
  • Creating shared tool banks
  • Setting up centralized artisan workshops
  • Funding technical assistance
  • Hiring trainers for capacity-building workshops
  • Product design training
  • Marketing training
  • Business management training
  • Technical skills development
  • Purchasing digital cameras
  • Internet connectivity costs
  • Website development

The goal is to improve production efficiency while enhancing artisans’ technical and business capabilities.

2. Marketing Support Grant

This category is designed for artisan groups that already have established production systems and existing sales.

The grant helps organizations expand into new markets and attract additional customers.

Funding may be used for:

  • Craft fair participation
  • Exhibition stand costs
  • Display shelving
  • Marketing brochures
  • Product catalogs
  • Promotional materials
  • Market expansion initiatives
  • Launching new products
  • Entering new domestic or international markets

This category is ideal for organizations ready to grow beyond their current customer base.

Eligibility Criteria

To qualify, applicants must satisfy all eligibility requirements.

Eligible Applicants

The program primarily supports established craft-producing organizations such as:

  • Artisan cooperatives
  • Community-based organizations
  • Small artisan businesses
  • Craft-producing groups

The primary focus is on organizations located in developing countries.

Eligible Craft Products

Supported products must:

  • Be handmade
  • Have artistic value
  • Serve a functional purpose
  • Reflect cultural significance

Applicants must submit photographs demonstrating the products.

Recommendation Letter Requirement

Every application must include a:

  • Signed recommendation letter
  • Dated recommendation letter

The recommendation should come from an organization familiar with the applicant’s work.

Suitable organizations include:

  • Previous funding organizations
  • Development partners
  • National craft organizations
  • International nonprofit organizations
  • Organizations with direct knowledge of the artisan group

The recommendation must be credible and independently written.

Applications without a signed and dated recommendation letter will automatically be rejected.

Important notes include:

  • Letters from previous Canvas Home Small Grant recipients are not accepted.
  • Recommendation letters must not be purchased.
  • Recommenders must not charge fees.

Language Requirements

Applications must:

  • Be written entirely in English
  • Use only U.S. Dollar (USD) currency

Photo Requirements

Applicants must submit:

  • At least five recent photographs
  • Photos taken within the previous twelve months

The photographs should clearly showcase the artisan products.

Follow-Up Reporting Requirement

Grant recipients agree to submit a follow-up report approximately six months after receiving the grant.

This report helps HtoM evaluate:

  • Grant impact
  • Business growth
  • Project implementation
  • Outcomes achieved

Banking Requirements

Applicants must have access to a bank account capable of receiving:

  • International wire transfers
  • U.S. Dollar (USD) payments

Recipients are responsible for:

  • Currency conversion fees
  • Bank transaction charges
  • Any costs associated with receiving grant funds

Selection Priorities

Because funding is limited, HtoM gives preference to applications that:

  • Provide detailed project explanations
  • Include strong justifications for funding
  • Benefit large numbers of artisans
  • Promote cultural preservation
  • Demonstrate environmental sustainability
  • Clearly explain financial need
  • Present a realistic implementation plan
  • Identify specific markets for finished products
  • Have reliable communication contacts
  • Can provide regular project updates
  • Have never previously received a Canvas Home Small Grant

Although previous recipients may apply, first-time applicants receive preference.

Activities Not Supported

Canvas Home Small Grants will not fund:

  • Individual artisans
  • New craft start-up projects
  • Building trade projects
  • Industrial or household products lacking handmade artistic value
  • Salaries
  • Staff wages
  • Travel expenses
  • Vehicle purchases
  • Environmentally harmful production practices
  • Projects using uncertified timber from sensitive ecosystems
  • Independent grant writers working without close relationships with artisan groups
  • Designers or exporters who do not directly work with artisans

Applicants should carefully review these exclusions before applying.

Application Timeline

The application process follows the schedule below.

Activity Date
Applications Open July 15, 2026
Application Deadline August 17, 2026 (11:59 PM EST)
Internal Review August 2026
Award Notifications September 2026
Grant Payments First week of October 2026

Late or incomplete applications will not be reviewed.

Application Process

Applicants should complete every section of the online application carefully.

The review process includes two stages.

Stage One

HtoM staff verify:

  • Eligibility
  • Required documents
  • Completeness of the application

Staff do not evaluate project quality during this stage.

Stage Two

Eligible applications are forwarded to an independent review committee responsible for selecting grant recipients.

Applicants will receive notification by email whether they are selected or not.

Successful applicants will receive funding after confirming their banking information.

Required Documents

Applicants should prepare:

  • Completed online application
  • Signed recommendation letter
  • Five recent product photographs
  • Supporting documentation requested within the application

Preparing these materials before beginning the application can make the submission process easier.

How to Apply

Interested organizations should submit their applications through the official Canvas Home Small Grants Application Form.

Applicants are encouraged to:

  1. Preview all application questions before starting.
  2. Review the Canvas Home Small Grants Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ).
  3. Prepare all required supporting documents.
  4. Create a free Jotform account to save progress and return later if necessary.
  5. Submit the completed application before the deadline.

Applicants unable to complete the online application due to technical issues may request a PDF application by contacting:

RachelL@creativelearning.org

However, PDF submissions are accepted only with prior approval from HANDMADE to MARKET.

For technical support during the application process, applicants should also contact the same email address.

APPLY HERE

Why You Should Apply

The Canvas Home Small Grants Program provides much more than financial assistance.

It enables artisan organizations to:

  • Increase production capacity
  • Preserve traditional craftsmanship
  • Improve product quality
  • Strengthen business operations
  • Reach international markets
  • Build sustainable livelihoods
  • Create employment opportunities
  • Promote environmentally responsible production
  • Expand income-generating opportunities
  • Strengthen community development

For artisan organizations seeking practical funding to support growth while preserving cultural heritage, this grant represents an excellent opportunity.

Visit HERE to learn more about the Canvas Home Small Grants 2026 for Artisan Groups in Developing Countries

Important Deadline

Application Deadline: August 17, 2026, at 11:59 PM Eastern Standard Time (EST).

Applications submitted after the deadline or missing required documentation will not be considered.

Conclusion

The Canvas Home Small Grants 2026 program offers a valuable opportunity for established artisan organizations in developing countries to strengthen their businesses through targeted financial support. With grants of up to $1,500, eligible groups can invest in better materials, equipment, technical training, marketing initiatives, and business development activities that improve productivity and expand market opportunities.

Beyond financial assistance, the program emphasizes cultural preservation, environmental sustainability, and long-term community development, making it an important initiative for artisan groups committed to creating lasting economic impact. Interested organizations should begin preparing their applications, recommendation letters, product photographs, and supporting documents early to ensure they submit a complete application before the August 17, 2026 deadline.

For more global Grant opportunities visit OFY HERE

The post Canvas Home Small Grants 2026 for Artisan Groups in Developing Countries | Up to $1,500 in Funding for Craft Businesses and Cooperatives appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: European Commission OCT Youth Network 2026–2027: Fully Funded One-Year Leadership Opportunity for Young People from Overseas Countries and Territories
item.dc:creator: Fridah Kamuri
item.description:
The European Commission has officially opened applications for the 2026–2027 Overseas Countries and Territories (OCT) Youth Network, offering an exceptional one-year leadership and learning opportunity for young people from the 13 Overseas Countries and Territories associated with the European Union. Designed to strengthen the partnership between the European Union and the Overseas Countries and Territories, […]

The post European Commission OCT Youth Network 2026–2027: Fully Funded One-Year Leadership Opportunity for Young People from Overseas Countries and Territories appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The European Commission has officially opened applications for the 2026–2027 Overseas Countries and Territories (OCT) Youth Network, offering an exceptional one-year leadership and learning opportunity for young people from the 13 Overseas Countries and Territories associated with the European Union.

Designed to strengthen the partnership between the European Union and the Overseas Countries and Territories, the OCT Youth Network equips young leaders with knowledge about European institutions, policymaking, sustainable development, and international cooperation while creating lasting connections among youth from different territories.

Participants will embark on an engaging one-year journey that combines study visits, leadership dialogues, networking events, online learning sessions, and participation in international forums. The programme empowers young people to represent the voices of their communities while contributing fresh ideas to the future of the OCT-EU Partnership.

If you are passionate about leadership, international affairs, youth participation, diplomacy, public policy, sustainable development, or European cooperation, this programme offers an invaluable opportunity to broaden your horizons and become part of an influential international youth community.

What is the OCT Youth Network?

The Overseas Countries and Territories Youth Network is a prestigious initiative established by the European Commission to strengthen engagement between young people living in Overseas Countries and Territories and the European Union.

The Network consists of 25 carefully selected young people between the ages of 20 and 28, representing all thirteen Overseas Countries and Territories associated with the EU.

Since its launch in 2022, the programme has become one of the flagship youth engagement initiatives supporting stronger collaboration between the European Union and its Overseas Countries and Territories.

Rather than being a traditional training programme, the Network serves as a platform where young people actively contribute ideas, exchange experiences, influence dialogue, and represent youth perspectives within the OCT-EU Partnership.

Members spend an entire year participating in collaborative learning activities while building professional relationships with policymakers, EU institutions, fellow youth leaders, and experts from across the territories.

Purpose of the OCT Youth Network

The initiative was created to increase youth participation in international cooperation and ensure that young voices play an important role in shaping the future of the OCT-EU Partnership.

Its primary objectives include:

  • Connecting young people from all 13 Overseas Countries and Territories.
  • Building stronger relationships between OCT youth and European Union institutions.
  • Increasing awareness and understanding of the European Union across the OCTs.
  • Giving young people opportunities to contribute directly to important policy discussions.
  • Promoting dialogue between youth and political decision-makers.
  • Creating stronger links between OCT youth initiatives and other regional, national, and European youth programmes.
  • Encouraging intercultural understanding among young people from diverse backgrounds.
  • Supporting meaningful youth participation in sustainable development.

What Participants Will Experience

Successful applicants become members of a dynamic international network and participate in numerous educational and leadership activities throughout the year.

The programme includes:

1. Study Visit to Brussels

One of the highlights of the programme is a week-long study visit to Brussels, Belgium.

During this visit, members will:

  • Explore how European institutions function.
  • Visit major EU institutions.
  • Learn how European policies are developed.
  • Meet European policymakers.
  • Participate in interactive workshops.
  • Engage in structured dialogue sessions.
  • Exchange ideas with European leaders.
  • Learn more about the OCT-EU Partnership.

The Brussels experience gives participants firsthand exposure to European governance and international cooperation.

2. Online Learning Sessions

Throughout the year, members participate in various virtual learning activities including:

  • Interactive webinars.
  • Capacity-building workshops.
  • Educational seminars.
  • Skills development sessions.
  • Policy discussions.
  • Expert presentations.

These sessions deepen participants’ understanding of:

  • European institutions
  • International partnerships
  • Sustainable development
  • Youth participation
  • Leadership
  • Governance
  • Public policy

3. Networking Opportunities

Participants become part of a growing international youth community.

Networking opportunities include:

  • Connecting with youth leaders from all 13 OCTs.
  • Building relationships with EU institutions.
  • Meeting government representatives.
  • Engaging with international organisations.
  • Connecting with policymakers.
  • Collaborating with experts and stakeholders.
  • Building lifelong friendships and professional relationships.

4. Participation in International Events

Network members participate in several prestigious international events.

These may include:

  • OCT-EU Forum
  • Climate-related conferences
  • European Youth events
  • International policy dialogues
  • High-level stakeholder meetings

Participation allows young leaders to share perspectives from their territories while contributing to important international discussions.

5. Youth Representation

Members play an active role in ensuring young people’s voices are heard.

They contribute by:

  • Presenting youth recommendations.
  • Participating in dialogue sessions.
  • Co-leading discussions.
  • Helping identify priorities for the Network.
  • Sharing experiences from their territories.
  • Promoting youth perspectives during high-level events.

Benefits of Joining the OCT Youth Network

Participants gain numerous academic, professional, and personal benefits throughout the programme.

These include:

  • One-year international leadership experience.
  • Participation in a study visit to Brussels.
  • Direct exposure to European institutions.
  • Access to EU policymakers and decision-makers.
  • Expanded international professional network.
  • Opportunities to represent youth interests.
  • Participation in international conferences and forums.
  • Leadership development.
  • Improved communication skills.
  • Greater understanding of international cooperation.
  • Cross-cultural learning experiences.
  • Collaboration with young leaders from different territories.
  • Enhanced knowledge of European governance.
  • Personal growth and increased global awareness.

Diversity and Inclusion

The European Commission emphasizes diversity within the Network.

The OCT Youth Network welcomes applicants from a broad range of backgrounds, including individuals with different:

  • Educational qualifications.
  • Professional experiences.
  • Areas of expertise.
  • Social backgrounds.
  • Cultural identities.
  • Personal interests.
  • Economic circumstances.

This diversity strengthens dialogue and promotes inclusive decision-making.

History of the OCT Youth Network

First Edition (2022–2023)

The inaugural Network brought together 25 young people representing 10 Overseas Countries and Territories.

One of its major achievements was the development of a Youth Declaration, which was presented during the OCT-EU Forum in Nouméa, New Caledonia in November 2022.

The declaration highlighted young people’s priorities regarding the OCT-EU Partnership.

Second Edition (2023–2024)

The second cohort included representatives from all 13 OCTs.

Members participated in:

  • Study visits to Brussels.
  • Networking activities.
  • Policy discussions.
  • Cultural exchanges.

A major achievement was the presentation of:

  • A Youth Declaration.
  • A photo exhibition.
  • A video exhibition.

These showcased the importance of cultural diversity and stronger exchanges between the OCTs and the European Union during the OCT-EU Forum held in Brussels in February 2024.

Third Edition (2024–2025)

The third cohort continued expanding the Network’s impact.

Highlights included:

  • Structured dialogue with Younous Omarjee, Vice-President of the European Parliament responsible for OCT matters.
  • Participation in the Climate Finance Forum in Brussels.
  • Attendance at the European Youth Event in Strasbourg.
  • Representation at the United Nations Ocean Conference in Nice.

These experiences demonstrated the growing influence of OCT youth within international discussions.

Why the European Union Created the OCT Youth Network

The initiative reflects the European Union’s increasing commitment to youth participation in global development.

It aligns closely with the Youth Action Plan in EU External Action, adopted in October 2022.

The Action Plan recognizes young people as key partners in building:

  • Democratic societies.
  • Inclusive communities.
  • Peaceful societies.
  • Sustainable development.
  • Strong international partnerships.

The OCT Youth Network is one practical way the European Union is turning this commitment into action.

How to Apply

Applications are submitted through the official European Commission application process.

Interested candidates should carefully review the eligibility requirements and complete the application before the deadline.

Applicants are encouraged to prepare thoughtful responses that demonstrate:

  • Leadership potential.
  • Motivation.
  • Interest in international cooperation.
  • Commitment to representing young people from their territory.
  • Enthusiasm for contributing to the OCT-EU Partnership.

APPLY HERE

Application Deadline

30 August 2026 (23:59 CEST)

Late applications are not expected to be considered.

Why You Should Apply

The OCT Youth Network is much more than a youth programme—it is a gateway to international leadership, diplomacy, policymaking, and global collaboration.

Participants gain unique access to European institutions, engage directly with policymakers, represent young people from their territories, and build lasting international friendships while contributing meaningfully to the future of the OCT-EU Partnership.

For young people seeking to develop leadership skills, expand their international network, and play an active role in shaping youth engagement between the European Union and the Overseas Countries and Territories, this programme offers an exceptional platform for personal and professional growth.

Visit HERE to learn more about the European Commission OCT Youth Network 2026–2027

Conclusion

The European Commission’s 2026–2027 Overseas Countries and Territories Youth Network presents an outstanding opportunity for ambitious young leaders from the 13 Overseas Countries and Territories associated with the European Union. Through a comprehensive one-year programme featuring study visits, online learning, policy engagement, international networking, and participation in prestigious global events, selected participants will gain invaluable knowledge, leadership experience, and lifelong professional connections. If you meet the eligibility requirements and are passionate about representing your community while contributing to the future of the OCT-EU Partnership, this is an opportunity not to miss.

Application Deadline: 30 August 2026 (23:59 CEST)

For more global Fellowship opportunities, visit OFY HERE 

The post European Commission OCT Youth Network 2026–2027: Fully Funded One-Year Leadership Opportunity for Young People from Overseas Countries and Territories appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Leo’s Experimental Micrograntting Program 2026 | $10,000 Grants for Bold Ideas to Help Humanity Navigate AGI
item.dc:creator: Evelyn Essuman
item.description:
Have an ambitious idea that could genuinely help humanity navigate the future of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)? Leo’s Experimental Microgranting Program offers a refreshing alternative to traditional funding by providing $10,000 microgrants with minimal bureaucracy and maximum flexibility. Unlike conventional grants that require lengthy proposals and institutional affiliations, this initiative encourages applicants to spend about […]

The post Leo’s Experimental Micrograntting Program 2026 | $10,000 Grants for Bold Ideas to Help Humanity Navigate AGI appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Have an ambitious idea that could genuinely help humanity navigate the future of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)? Leo’s Experimental Microgranting Program offers a refreshing alternative to traditional funding by providing $10,000 microgrants with minimal bureaucracy and maximum flexibility.

Unlike conventional grants that require lengthy proposals and institutional affiliations, this initiative encourages applicants to spend about an hour explaining a thoughtful idea. The emphasis is on originality, genuine reasoning, and ambitious thinking rather than polished grant-writing or impressive credentials.

Leo’s program is an experimental grantmaking initiative designed to discover unconventional projects that could contribute to ensuring AGI benefits humanity.

About the Program

The Experimental Microgranting Program is built around one simple principle:

Actually try.

Applicants are encouraged to submit thoughtful, ambitious proposals instead of incremental academic work or applications filled with buzzwords.

Leo believes promising ideas can come from anyone and not only professors or established researchers – wants to support projects that traditional grantmakers may overlook.

The program is intentionally lightweight:

  • Approximately one-hour application
  • Maximum 1,000 words
  • Minimal bureaucracy
  • Flexible funding
  • Fast, independent decision-making

Grant Value

Successful applicants receive:

  • $10,000 USD
  • Flexible funding
  • Support for individuals, nonprofits, charities, or other entities
  • Opportunity to reapply after project completion

What Projects Are Being Funded?

The program seeks projects that could positively influence humanity’s future with AGI.

Examples include:

  • AI alignment research
  • AI governance ideas
  • Technical safety tools
  • Educational initiatives
  • Public awareness projects
  • Novel research
  • Policy work
  • Community initiatives
  • Experimental approaches with high upside

Applicants are encouraged to justify how their proposal contributes to helping humanity navigate AGI.

What Makes This Grant Different?

Unlike many traditional grants, this program offers:

  • Anonymous funder
  • Flexible use of funding
  • Learning is considered a successful outcome
  • Supports unconventional ideas
  • Values original thinking over credentials
  • Minimal administrative burden

The initiative itself is also an experiment and may evolve over time.

Application Guidelines

Applicants should carefully read the complete guidance before applying.

Important expectations include:

  • Spend around one hour preparing the application.
  • Write clearly.
  • Explain why the project matters.
  • Avoid exaggerated claims.
  • Avoid unnecessary buzzwords.
  • Submit genuine work.
  • Applications written or edited by AI will be rejected.
  • Multiple ideas may be submitted separately.

Leo specifically emphasizes that applications should reflect the applicant’s own reasoning and writing.

Eligibility

The program welcomes applicants from diverse backgrounds.

Applicants do not need:

  • Previous AI alignment experience
  • Academic publications
  • Prestigious university affiliations
  • Extensive grant-writing experience

Strong ideas and thoughtful reasoning matter far more than credentials.

Funding Philosophy

Rather than expecting every project to succeed, the program views experimentation as valuable.

Even projects that produce negative results may still be considered successful if they generate meaningful learning.

Once grant funds have been used, recipients may apply again by providing a progress update.

Referral Bonus

Applicants who refer others can receive a $100 referral bounty if the referred applicant is awarded a microgrant.

Important Notes

Applicants should note:

  • Grants should not be used as credentials or endorsements.
  • Leo may seek external reviewers.
  • Grant decisions are highly selective.
  • Funding availability is expected to support all successful applications.
  • The program operates independently from Leo’s employer.

How to Apply

Applicants should first read the complete guidance document before completing the application form.

Application Form:
https://tally.so/r/D4WlbR

Questions:
alignmentmicrogrants@gmail.com

Conclusion

Leo’s Experimental Microgranting Program offers a refreshing alternative to traditional grant funding by placing trust in bold ideas, genuine reasoning, and intellectual curiosity rather than lengthy proposals or prestigious credentials. With a straightforward application process and flexible $10,000 funding, the program encourages individuals and organizations to pursue ambitious projects that could contribute to a safer and more beneficial future with Artificial General Intelligence (AGI).

Whether your proposal focuses on AI safety, governance, education, public engagement, technical research, or another innovative approach, this initiative welcomes unconventional thinking and recognizes that meaningful progress often begins with experimental ideas. Applicants are encouraged to write honestly, explain why their project matters, and demonstrate their own original thinking without relying on polished grant-writing or AI-generated content.

If you have a well-considered idea that could help humanity navigate the opportunities and challenges of AGI, this microgrant program provides an excellent opportunity to secure funding and turn that vision into action. Be sure to read the full application guidance carefully before applying, follow all instructions, and submit your proposal through the official application form. Even if your project does not achieve every intended outcome, the program values learning and meaningful insights, making it an ideal opportunity for innovators who are willing to experiment, take thoughtful risks, and contribute to shaping a positive future for humanity.

Discover more opportunities like these on the OFY webpage .

The post Leo’s Experimental Micrograntting Program 2026 | $10,000 Grants for Bold Ideas to Help Humanity Navigate AGI appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth
item.dc:creator: Fridah Kamuri
item.description:
Education Cannot Wait (ECW), the United Nations global fund dedicated to education in emergencies and protracted crises, has officially opened applications for the 2026 ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election Call. This unique global opportunity invites eligible youth-led and student-led organizations to become part of an influential international constituency that helps shape ECW’s […]

The post Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Education Cannot Wait (ECW), the United Nations global fund dedicated to education in emergencies and protracted crises, has officially opened applications for the 2026 ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election Call. This unique global opportunity invites eligible youth-led and student-led organizations to become part of an influential international constituency that helps shape ECW’s governance, policies, and strategic priorities.

Far more than a standard membership opportunity, the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group provides youth organizations with a direct platform to influence global decision-making in education, humanitarian response, and crisis recovery. Selected organizations will have opportunities to contribute to policy consultations, engage with international education stakeholders, vote in governance elections, and even seek election to leadership positions within ECW’s governance structure.

For youth organizations committed to advancing education for children and young people affected by conflict, displacement, disasters, and other crises, this represents an exceptional opportunity to amplify their voices on the global stage while helping shape the future of education in emergencies.

What is the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group?

The ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group is a global constituency established to ensure that the voices, experiences, and priorities of young people and crisis-affected communities are reflected throughout Education Cannot Wait’s governance and decision-making processes.

Recognizing that young people are not merely beneficiaries of education programmes but active partners in designing solutions, ECW created this platform to strengthen youth participation in shaping international education policies and investments.

The Sub-group brings together youth-led and student-led organizations from around the world that are actively working in education, humanitarian response, and crisis contexts. Through this network, members contribute valuable perspectives that influence ECW’s policies, governance, and strategic direction.

Why This Opportunity Matters

Millions of children and young people worldwide face interrupted education because of armed conflict, climate change, forced displacement, political instability, and humanitarian emergencies.

Education Cannot Wait works to ensure that quality education remains accessible during these crises. However, sustainable solutions require meaningful participation from the young people and organizations directly affected by these challenges.

The Youth and Student-led Sub-group ensures that youth voices remain central to discussions about:

  • Education in emergencies.
  • Humanitarian response.
  • Recovery from crises.
  • Inclusive education policies.
  • Long-term resilience.
  • Youth participation in governance.
  • Global education financing.

By joining the Sub-group, organizations become active contributors to international conversations that influence education policy worldwide.

Objectives of the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group

The initiative aims to:

  • Strengthen youth representation within ECW governance.
  • Ensure crisis-affected communities influence policy decisions.
  • Promote meaningful youth participation in international education discussions.
  • Build stronger collaboration between youth organizations and global education partners.
  • Increase accountability through inclusive decision-making.
  • Foster partnerships among youth-led organizations working in emergency education.
  • Support leadership development within the global education sector.

Benefits of Joining the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group

Successful organizations receive access to numerous professional, networking, and governance opportunities.

These include:

1. Participate in Global Policy Consultations

Members contribute directly to consultations regarding ECW’s:

  • Strategic priorities.
  • Policies.
  • Programmes.
  • Governance decisions.
  • Future initiatives.

Their perspectives help ensure that youth experiences remain central to global education planning.

2. Influence International Decision-Making

One of the most valuable aspects of membership is the opportunity to influence international education governance.

Members provide recommendations and insights that shape ECW’s work supporting children and youth affected by crises.

3. Build International Networks

Organizations become part of a worldwide community of professionals and youth organizations working in:

  • Education.
  • Humanitarian assistance.
  • Emergency response.
  • Child protection.
  • Youth leadership.
  • Community development.

These connections encourage collaboration and knowledge sharing across regions.

4. Participate in Governance Elections

Members have voting rights in elections for representatives serving on ECW governance bodies.

This enables organizations to participate in selecting leaders who will represent youth interests within the institution.

5. Opportunity to Seek Leadership Positions

Eligible organizations may not only vote but also stand for election.

During the 2026 membership cycle, organizations may seek election to represent the constituency on:

  • High-Level Steering Group (HLSG).
  • Executive Committee (ExCom).

Successful candidates will serve a two-year mandate, representing youth voices within ECW’s highest governance structures.

Who Can Apply?

Applications are open to eligible organizations that meet ECW’s membership requirements.

Applicants must be:

  • Youth-led organizations.
  • Student-led organizations.
  • Not-for-profit organizations.
  • Organizations working in education.
  • Organizations supporting education in emergencies.
  • Organizations working within humanitarian or crisis settings.

Applicants should carefully review the official Membership & Election Guidance Note for complete eligibility criteria before submitting their application.

Leadership Opportunities Available

Membership also provides organizations with the opportunity to play a leadership role within ECW.

Representatives elected to the High-Level Steering Group or Executive Committee contribute to:

  • Strategic planning.
  • Governance oversight.
  • Representation of youth interests.
  • Policy discussions.
  • Organizational accountability.

These leadership roles help ensure that youth-led organizations have meaningful influence over ECW’s future direction.

Key Dates

Applicants should take note of the important deadlines:

  • Membership applications open: 6 July 2026
  • Deadline for membership applications and election nominations: 26 July 2026
  • Voting period: 3–5 August 2026
  • Announcement of elected representatives: 7 August 2026

How to Apply

Eligible organizations should complete the official application process by following these steps:

  1. Complete the Membership Application Form.
  2. Submit the Election Nomination Form if seeking election.
  3. Download and carefully review the Membership & Election Guidance Note.
  4. Ensure all eligibility requirements are met before submission.
  5. Submit the application before the deadline.

APPLY HERE 

Why Organizations Should Apply

Membership in the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group offers far more than recognition. It provides organizations with a genuine opportunity to shape international education governance while representing the interests of young people affected by crises around the world.

Participating organizations gain access to influential policy discussions, strengthen their international partnerships, contribute to global education solutions, and help ensure that youth perspectives remain central to humanitarian and development responses.

For organizations seeking to expand their impact beyond national borders, strengthen their leadership profile, and influence the future of education in emergencies, this opportunity offers an exceptional platform for meaningful engagement.

Visit HERE to learn more about the Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026

Application Deadline

26 July 2026

Applications and election nominations must be submitted by this deadline.

Conclusion

The Education Cannot Wait Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026 Membership and Election Call is a remarkable opportunity for eligible youth-led and student-led organizations committed to advancing education in emergencies and supporting crisis-affected communities. Through membership, organizations can actively participate in global policy consultations, contribute to ECW’s governance, build international partnerships, vote in leadership elections, and even stand for influential positions on the High-Level Steering Group and Executive Committee. If your organization is passionate about youth leadership, education, and humanitarian action, this is an opportunity to help shape the future of global education governance while ensuring that the voices of young people remain at the heart of decision-making.

Application Deadline: 26 July 2026.

For more global Fellowship opportunities, visit OFY HERE

The post Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals
item.dc:creator: Priyanka Kharbanda
item.description:
The Shorenstein Fellows Program at the Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy, housed within Harvard Kennedy School, is one of the world’s leading fellowship opportunities for professionals and scholars working at the intersection of media, technology, politics, and public policy. For nearly four decades, the program has served as a hub for influential […]

The post Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Shorenstein Fellows Program at the Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy, housed within Harvard Kennedy School, is one of the world’s leading fellowship opportunities for professionals and scholars working at the intersection of media, technology, politics, and public policy.

For nearly four decades, the program has served as a hub for influential thinkers, journalists, policymakers, technologists, filmmakers, creators, and researchers who are shaping how information is produced, distributed, and understood in democratic societies.

Founded in 1986, the fellowship has welcomed more than 300 distinguished fellows from around the world. These fellows have contributed groundbreaking research, influential publications, public discussions, documentaries, policy recommendations, and innovative projects that address some of the most pressing challenges affecting today’s global information ecosystem.

The Shorenstein Fellows Program offers selected participants the opportunity to pursue an independent project while becoming part of Harvard Kennedy School’s vibrant intellectual community. Fellows collaborate with leading scholars and practitioners while producing original work that informs public debate and strengthens democratic institutions.

Whether your expertise lies in journalism, artificial intelligence, technology policy, digital governance, media innovation, political communication, public leadership, or academic research, the Shorenstein Fellows Program provides a prestigious platform to expand your ideas and contribute to global conversations.

About the Shorenstein Center

The Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy is a research center at Harvard Kennedy School dedicated to studying how information shapes societies, governments, and democratic institutions.

Its work focuses on understanding:

  • Journalism and news media.
  • Digital technology.
  • Artificial intelligence.
  • Public policy.
  • Political communication.
  • Information integrity.
  • Democracy.
  • Civic engagement.
  • The evolving global information ecosystem.

Through research, fellowships, publications, events, and collaborations, the Center seeks to improve the creation, distribution, and consumption of trustworthy information worldwide.

About the Shorenstein Fellows Program

The Shorenstein Fellows Program is designed to bring together professionals and scholars from multiple disciplines who are interested in examining critical issues surrounding media, politics, technology, and governance.

The fellowship emphasizes independent scholarship while encouraging collaboration within Harvard’s academic environment.

Participants pursue self-directed projects that contribute meaningful insights into the changing landscape of information, democracy, and public communication.

Throughout the fellowship, fellows engage with faculty, researchers, students, and fellow practitioners while sharing expertise from their own professional backgrounds.

Purpose of the Fellowship

The fellowship aims to:

  • Advance public understanding of media and democracy.
  • Support innovative research on technology and public policy.
  • Encourage interdisciplinary collaboration.
  • Promote thoughtful analysis of today’s information ecosystem.
  • Foster dialogue between practitioners and academics.
  • Produce research that informs policymakers and the general public.
  • Strengthen democratic institutions through better understanding of information flows.

Areas of Focus

The fellowship welcomes projects exploring a broad range of topics, including:

  • Journalism
  • Digital media
  • Artificial intelligence
  • Social media
  • Information integrity
  • Public policy
  • Political communication
  • Democracy
  • Technology governance
  • Election integrity
  • Misinformation and disinformation
  • Civic engagement
  • Digital platforms
  • Media ethics
  • Public interest storytelling
  • Documentary filmmaking
  • Government innovation
  • Internet governance
  • Freedom of expression
  • Data and society

Interdisciplinary proposals that combine multiple fields are particularly encouraged.

What Fellows Do

During the fellowship, participants pursue an independent project while actively engaging with the Shorenstein Center community.

Projects may include:

  • Academic research
  • Policy papers
  • Opinion articles
  • Essays
  • Investigative journalism
  • Reports
  • Webinars
  • Documentary storytelling
  • Multimedia projects
  • Public presentations
  • Books or book chapters
  • Digital publications

The fellowship encourages work that translates complex ideas into accessible content for both policymakers and the general public.

Contribution to the Research Community

Beyond completing their own projects, fellows contribute to the broader intellectual life of the Center by:

  • Participating in discussions.
  • Sharing expertise with faculty and students.
  • Collaborating across disciplines.
  • Presenting research findings.
  • Engaging in seminars and public events.
  • Supporting dialogue around contemporary media and policy challenges.

This collaborative environment allows fellows to benefit from diverse perspectives while expanding the impact of their own work.

Distinguished Fellowship Community

Since its establishment, the Shorenstein Fellows Program has hosted an impressive community of professionals representing journalism, technology, government, academia, filmmaking, and policy.

The Center has welcomed over 300 fellows since 1986, reflecting its longstanding commitment to fostering thought leadership across multiple sectors.

Recent fellows have included:

  • Journalists
  • Documentary filmmakers
  • Artificial intelligence experts
  • Technology leaders
  • Public policy specialists
  • Government officials
  • Academic researchers
  • Digital innovation experts
  • Media executives

This diverse fellowship community creates valuable opportunities for networking, collaboration, and intellectual exchange.

Research and Publications

Fellows contribute to an extensive body of work addressing today’s most important challenges surrounding media and democracy.

Examples of publications include:

  • Research reports
  • Policy papers
  • Commentary articles
  • Explainers
  • Public webinars
  • Essays
  • Original journalism

Recent topics have explored:

  • Artificial intelligence in journalism
  • Media consumption
  • Government technology
  • Digital democracy
  • Information integrity
  • Emerging technologies
  • Public trust

These publications help inform policymakers, researchers, journalists, and the broader public.

Why the Fellowship Is Prestigious

The Shorenstein Fellows Program is internationally respected because it combines:

  • Harvard Kennedy School’s academic excellence.
  • Independent research opportunities.
  • Interdisciplinary collaboration.
  • Public policy engagement.
  • Global visibility.
  • Access to leading experts.
  • Opportunities to influence contemporary debates.

For professionals working in media, technology, journalism, or public policy, the fellowship represents an opportunity to significantly expand both research impact and professional networks.

Benefits of the Shorenstein Fellows Program

Although individual fellowship arrangements may vary, participants generally benefit from:

  • Affiliation with Harvard Kennedy School.
  • Membership in an internationally recognized research community.
  • Opportunities to pursue independent research.
  • Collaboration with distinguished scholars and practitioners.
  • Access to seminars, discussions, and academic events.
  • A platform to publish original work.
  • Increased visibility within media and policy circles.
  • Professional networking opportunities.
  • Intellectual exchange across multiple disciplines.
  • Long-term association with an influential fellowship network.

Who Should Consider Applying?

This fellowship is ideal for professionals who wish to explore important issues affecting today’s information ecosystem.

Strong candidates may include:

  • Journalists
  • Editors
  • Researchers
  • Professors
  • Public policy experts
  • Political advisors
  • Government officials
  • Technology professionals
  • AI researchers
  • Documentary filmmakers
  • Media entrepreneurs
  • Communication specialists
  • Civic technology leaders
  • Digital platform researchers

Apply here

Why This Fellowship Matters Today

The rapid evolution of technology, artificial intelligence, digital media, and political communication has transformed how societies access and evaluate information.

The Shorenstein Fellows Program supports research that addresses these changes while promoting evidence-based dialogue and innovative solutions.

By bringing together experts from diverse backgrounds, the fellowship encourages collaboration on issues that influence democratic governance, journalism, and public trust worldwide.

For more details please visit here 

Application Deadline

No application deadline is provided in the available information.

Prospective applicants should regularly monitor the Shorenstein Center’s official fellowship page for future application announcements, eligibility updates, and submission deadlines.

Conclusion

The Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program is one of the world’s premier fellowships for professionals and scholars dedicated to improving the understanding of media, technology, politics, and public policy. By providing an intellectually rich environment where independent research, interdisciplinary collaboration, and public engagement thrive, the fellowship empowers participants to produce meaningful work with lasting societal impact. With its distinguished global network, longstanding reputation, and commitment to advancing knowledge about today’s evolving information ecosystem, the program offers an exceptional opportunity for journalists, technologists, policymakers, scholars, and creators seeking to shape the future of media and democracy. Interested applicants should monitor the official fellowship announcements for future application deadlines and program updates.

For more fellowship opportunities please visit OFY here

The post Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Methodology PhD Studentships in Maternity Disparities 2027 in the UK: Fully Funded NIHR PhD Opportunities for Researchers Passionate About Maternal Health Equity
item.dc:creator: Richard Nyirenda
item.description:
Persistent inequalities in maternity care continue to affect the health and wellbeing of women and babies across the United Kingdom. Addressing these disparities requires innovative research, skilled researchers, and multidisciplinary collaboration. To help build the next generation of experts in this critical field, the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) Inequalities Challenge: Maternity […]

The post Methodology PhD Studentships in Maternity Disparities 2027 in the UK: Fully Funded NIHR PhD Opportunities for Researchers Passionate About Maternal Health Equity appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Persistent inequalities in maternity care continue to affect the health and wellbeing of women and babies across the United Kingdom. Addressing these disparities requires innovative research, skilled researchers, and multidisciplinary collaboration. To help build the next generation of experts in this critical field, the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) Inequalities Challenge: Maternity Disparities Consortium (MDC) has launched its Methodology PhD Studentships Competition for 2027.

The Consortium is offering up to 13 fully funded PhD studentships across multiple universities within its UK-wide network. These studentships are designed to develop future research leaders who can generate evidence-based solutions that reduce inequalities before, during, and after pregnancy.

This opportunity is particularly suitable for applicants with expertise in research methodologies such as statistics, health economics, implementation science, digital research, data science, trial design, co-production methods, and related disciplines who are passionate about improving maternal health outcomes.

Whether you are an aspiring academic, healthcare professional, statistician, economist, data scientist, or researcher from another methodological background, this programme provides an exceptional opportunity to contribute to meaningful research while earning a fully funded doctoral degree.

About the NIHR Inequalities Challenge: Maternity Disparities Consortium (MDC)

The Maternity Disparities Consortium (MDC) is a major UK-wide collaboration established under the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) to address longstanding inequalities in maternity care.

The Consortium brings together a diverse partnership of:

  • Universities
  • NHS Trusts
  • Local councils
  • Charities
  • Industry partners
  • Research organisations

By combining expertise from these sectors, the Consortium aims to develop practical, evidence-based solutions that improve maternity outcomes for women and babies throughout pregnancy and beyond.

The Consortium is committed to conducting inclusive research that reflects the experiences of diverse communities while ensuring that research findings lead to real improvements in healthcare services.

Why This PhD Programme Matters

Despite significant advances in healthcare, inequalities in maternity outcomes remain a major challenge in the UK. Certain communities continue to experience poorer maternal and neonatal outcomes due to social, economic, ethnic, and healthcare-related disparities.

The Methodology PhD Studentships aim to strengthen research capacity by training specialists who can develop innovative research methods capable of addressing these complex issues.

Successful students will become part of a national research community working towards improving maternal health through rigorous scientific investigation and interdisciplinary collaboration.

Capacity Development Programme

A central objective of the Maternity Disparities Consortium is to build sustainable research capacity across the United Kingdom.

The Consortium’s Capacity Development Programme seeks to:

  • Develop the next generation of maternity disparities researchers.
  • Build long-term expertise in methodological research.
  • Strengthen the UK’s maternity research workforce.
  • Support future research leaders.
  • Encourage collaboration across healthcare, academic, clinical, and community settings.
  • Increase research participation from underrepresented disciplines.
  • Promote inclusive research environments.
  • Deliver lasting improvements in maternal healthcare.

The programme also contributes to important UK government priorities, including:

  • Women’s Health Strategy for England.
  • 10-Year Health Plan for England.

Through this initiative, the Consortium hopes to create a stronger, more diverse workforce capable of delivering meaningful improvements in maternal healthcare for years to come.

PhD Studentship Opportunities

The Consortium will fund up to 13 PhD studentships, each based at one of the participating universities within the Consortium.

Projects will focus on reducing maternity inequalities using advanced research methodologies.

Additional research projects are expected to be announced throughout the recruitment period.

Applicants are encouraged to review individual project descriptions before applying, as each project may have its own specific requirements.

Research Areas Being Sought

The Consortium is particularly interested in applicants with methodological expertise in areas such as:

  • Health Economics
  • Statistics
  • Trial Design
  • Data Science
  • Digital Research
  • Implementation Science
  • Co-production Methods

Applicants from other methodological backgrounds are also encouraged to apply if they believe their skills can contribute to maternity inequalities research.

The Consortium values interdisciplinary approaches and welcomes innovative ideas that can improve maternal and neonatal outcomes.

Programme Duration

The PhD studentships will begin in:

January 2027

Students may choose one of the following study options where available:

  • Full-time study for up to 3 years.
  • Part-time study (0.8 Whole-Time Equivalent) for up to 4 years.

Some research projects may only be offered on a full-time basis. Applicants should carefully check the requirements for each individual project before submitting an application.

Funding Package

Successful applicants will receive a comprehensive funding package that includes:

Full Tuition Fees

The studentship covers:

  • Full UK home tuition fees at the 2026/27 rate.

Annual Tax-Free Stipend

Students will receive a tax-free maintenance stipend at UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) rates:

  • £21,805 per year for students based outside London.
  • £23,805 per year for students based in London.

These rates apply from 1 October 2026.

Research Support Funding

Each award includes:

  • £5,000 towards:
    • Research expenses
    • Training
    • Travel
    • Conference attendance
    • Consumables

This additional funding enables students to undertake high-quality research while developing their professional skills.

International Student Information

International applicants are welcome to apply.

However, applicants should note that:

  • The studentship only covers tuition fees at the UK Home rate.
  • International students must independently secure funding to cover the difference between Home and Overseas tuition fees.
  • Students must satisfy admission requirements set by their host university.

Applicants are advised to consult the postgraduate admissions requirements of their chosen university before applying.

Who Should Apply?

The Consortium welcomes applications from individuals who are passionate about improving maternal health equity through high-quality research.

Applicants should have methodological knowledge or experience relevant to one or more of the research areas being supported.

The Consortium is especially interested in individuals who can contribute fresh perspectives and innovative research approaches.

Applicants from diverse academic and professional backgrounds are encouraged to demonstrate how their previous experience will strengthen maternity inequalities research.

Where this is not immediately apparent, applicants should clearly explain in their cover letter how their disciplinary expertise is relevant to their proposed PhD project.

APPLY HERE

Commitment to Equality, Diversity and Inclusion

The Consortium is committed to creating an inclusive, supportive and caring research environment.

Applications are particularly encouraged from:

  • People from underrepresented ethnic backgrounds.
  • People living with disabilities.
  • Individuals with neurodivergent conditions.
  • People with long-term health requirements.
  • Applicants of all genders.
  • Applicants of all ages.
  • Applicants of all beliefs.

By promoting diversity within its research community, the Consortium aims to produce research that better reflects the populations it serves.

Why You Should Consider Applying

This studentship offers numerous benefits, including:

  • Fully funded doctoral study.
  • Tax-free annual living allowance.
  • Additional research funding.
  • Opportunities to collaborate with leading UK universities and NHS organisations.
  • Participation in nationally significant health research.
  • Access to multidisciplinary research expertise.
  • Professional development and advanced research training.
  • Opportunities to become a future leader in maternal health research.
  • Contribution to reducing inequalities affecting women and babies.

Important Information for Applicants

Before applying, applicants should remember to:

  • Review individual PhD project descriptions.
  • Confirm eligibility with the host university.
  • Check postgraduate admission requirements.
  • Prepare a strong cover letter explaining their methodological expertise.
  • Demonstrate how their experience aligns with maternity disparities research.
  • Verify funding arrangements if applying as an international student.

Deadline

No application deadline has been announced yet.

Applicants are encouraged to monitor the Consortium’s official announcements regularly, as additional PhD projects will continue to be added and application timelines may vary by host university.

Visit HERE to learn more about the Methodology PhD Studentships in Maternity Disparities 2027 in the UK

Conclusion

The NIHR Inequalities Challenge: Maternity Disparities Consortium Methodology PhD Studentships 2027 represent an outstanding opportunity for aspiring researchers who want to make a meaningful contribution to maternal health equity. With up to 13 fully funded doctoral positions, generous financial support, and access to a nationwide network of leading universities, NHS Trusts, councils, charities, and industry partners, this programme provides an exceptional platform for developing advanced research skills while addressing one of the UK’s most important public health challenges.

The Consortium is not only investing in doctoral education but also building a diverse and inclusive research community capable of delivering long-term improvements in maternity care. If you have expertise in research methodology and a passion for improving outcomes for women and babies, this programme offers an excellent opportunity to develop your academic career while creating lasting social impact.
